region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughal cuisine tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp food product class. No specific tandoori prawn entry exists; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers shrimps and prawns as a commodity group. No specific tandoori-marinated prawn entry exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent commodity-level match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for tandoori prawns. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared ethnic seafood dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tandoori prawns in Singapore consists of approximately 6 to 8 medium-to-large tiger prawns (about 250 g total), served with mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked, moist heat (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 15152) adapted with marinade components from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for tandoori spice and yogurt marinade contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy) and Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for tandoori prawns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA databases. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152 / FDC 175180) as the primary protein-fat-energy anchor, with marinade contribution (yogurt, cream, spices, oil) estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods 2017 (Table: Milk and Milk Products for yogurt; Fats and Oils for oil). The marinade adds approximately 15–20 kcal per 100 g and modest fat from cream and oil. Sodium is elevated due to salt in the marinade. Values represent the finished marinated and grilled dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp / prawn (GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on low-carbohydrate seafood preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tandoori prawns exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Tandoori prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish (approximately 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g). The GI is estimated at 15 based on the analogous food category of cooked crustaceans and protein-dominant grilled seafood dishes, which consistently show very low GI values (GI 10–20) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 9.5) / 100 ≈ 1.4, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving ≈ 9.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade salt and spice blend
hypertension.guidance: Tandoori prawns contain approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1300 mg per restaurant serving), which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a glycemic load of approximately 1.4 per serving, making them a suitable protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from prawns and moderate saturated fat from cream marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol (approximately 152 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat; the cream-based marinade adds modest saturated fat, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderate portions and be mindful of the elevated sodium content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Prawns (Hindi: तंदूरी झींगा; Malay: Udang Tandoori; Chinese: 坦都里虾; Tamil: தந்தூரி இறால்) is a classic North Indian seafood dish in which large tiger prawns are marinated in a spiced yogurt and cream mixture — typically containing red chilli powder, turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char and slightly crisp exterior while keeping the prawn flesh juicy and tender inside. The dish is typically served with fresh mint chutney, sliced raw onion, and lemon wedges. In Singapore, tandoori prawns are a popular menu item at North Indian restaurants, often featuring large tiger prawns sourced locally or regionally. Nutritionally, tandoori prawns are a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and only 3.8 g carbohydrate, making it one of the more nutritionally favourable Indian restaurant options. The glycemic load per serving is very low (approximately 1.4), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to salt in the marinade (approximately 520 mg per 100 g). Prawns are a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawns?
Is tandoori prawns good for diabetics?
Is tandoori prawns high in sodium?
Can I eat tandoori prawns if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in tandoori prawns marinade?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for tandoori prawns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp as the primary anchor with marinade contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN reference values. The adaptation is methodologically sound and consistent with the dish composition, but direct analytical data for this specific preparation would be required for high confidence.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates: USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (FDC 175180) provides the protein, fat, and energy anchor; marinade components (yogurt, cream, oil, spices) are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 reference values. Direct laboratory analysis of this dish would improve confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the source databases; shrimp contain trace vitamin D but the value is not confirmed for the marinated and grilled preparation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero for a non-industrially processed seafood dish with no hydrogenated fats in the marinade. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~15) based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked crustaceans and protein-dominant grilled seafood; no direct GI measurement for tandoori prawns is available. 5. Sodium value (520 mg per 100 g) reflects a moderately salted restaurant-style marinade; actual sodium may vary by restaurant. 6. The food_name has been normalised to the canonical English form 'Tandoori Prawns'; the input name 'Gulzaar Tandoori Prawns' is a restaurant-specific branded name and is preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is flagged as likely true for North Indian Muslim-heritage restaurants but cannot be guaranteed for all four listed restaurants without individual certification verification. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp reference values and are not independently confirmed for this specific preparation.
